summary:
The [[event:36|Yangshao culture]] of the Yellow River valley (5000–3000 BC) produced some of the most visually striking pottery of Neolithic China. Yangshao ceramics are characterized by fine red or buff earthenware decorated with bold painted designs in black, red, and white — featuring fish, deer, human faces, geometric spirals, and stylized plant motifs. The pottery was hand-built using the coiling method and fired in open or simple pit kilns at temperatures sufficient to produce a hard, smooth surface. Different regional variants of Yangshao pottery show considerable stylistic diversity, reflecting the culture's broad geographic spread across Shaanxi, Henan, and Gansu provinces.

Yangshao pottery served both practical and ceremonial functions, with finer vessels apparently associated with burial ritual. The fish motif, common at earlier Yangshao sites such as Banpo near present-day Xi''an, may have carried cosmological or totemic significance. The technological and aesthetic traditions developed within Yangshao ceramics influenced subsequent Chinese pottery traditions, and some scholars see continuities extending into later Bronze Age decorative programs. The culture's pottery is the primary means by which its geographic range and chronological phases are defined by archaeologists.
